name: Wrentham Co-Operative Bank
description_short: Massachusetts-chartered cooperative bank offering personal and business banking services with FDIC and DIF deposit insurance protection and no stockholders.
description_long:
Wrentham Cooperative Bank is a Massachusetts-chartered cooperative bank structured as a member-owned institution with no stockholders. Founded as a cooperative financial institution, the bank operates under a mutual ownership model where depositors and borrowers are members rather than shareholders. The bank is regulated as a member of both the FDIC and DIF (Deposit Insurance Fund), providing dual deposit protection.

The bank offers comprehensive personal banking services including checking accounts with rewards (Kasasa Cash earning 4.00% APY on balances up to $10,000), savings and money market accounts, certificates of deposit, retirement plans, mortgage loans, personal loans, and home equity loans. On the business side, they provide business checking, business credit cards, merchant services, retirement planning, and various loan products. All customers have access to online banking, mobile banking, bill pay, eStatements, and Zelle money transfer services.

Wrentham distinguishes itself through its cooperative structure—a member-owned model that theoretically prioritizes member interests over shareholder profits. The bank emphasizes security through dual FDIC and DIF deposit insurance coverage ($250,000 each), exceeding standard FDIC protection. They offer competitive checking rates through Kasasa partnerships and maintain physical branch locations with extended service hours alongside modern digital banking infrastructure.

As a regional Massachusetts-based cooperative bank, Wrentham serves consumers seeking community-focused banking with digital convenience. The main caveat is that as a smaller regional institution, they likely have fewer ATM locations and branch networks compared to major national banks, and specific product terms, rates, and fees require visiting their website or contacting the bank directly as detailed rate information is not provided in available content. Many banks offer installment loans with fixed monthly payments, giving borrowers a clear payoff timeline.
services: Personal checking accounts with Kasasa Cash rewards (4.00% APY up to $10,000)
Savings accounts and money market accounts
Retirement plans and retirement planning services
Online and mobile banking with bill pay
pros: Dual deposit insurance—FDIC and DIF coverage ($250,000 each) exceeds standard protection limits
Kasasa Cash checking offers 4.00% APY on balances up to $10,000 for qualifying members
Member-owned cooperative structure with no stockholders, designed to prioritize member interests
Comprehensive digital banking including mobile app, online banking, bill pay, and Zelle transfers
Full-service offerings for both personal and business customers including mortgages, loans, and merchant services
Physical branch locations with online convenience combining community banking with modern technology
NMLS-registered institution (ID #627361) with transparent regulatory compliance
cons: As a regional Massachusetts bank, likely has limited ATM network and branch locations compared to national banks
Specific interest rates, fees, and product terms not disclosed on homepage—requires direct inquiry
Limited information about credit card rewards programs, specific loan terms, or competitive rate comparisons
No mention of investment products beyond basic retirement plans or wealth management services
Mobile app functionality details not provided; unclear what specific features are available versus competitors
best_for: Massachusetts residents seeking community-focused banking with cooperative ownership principles
Consumers interested in high-yield checking through Kasasa Cash (4% APY) for everyday accounts
Small business owners needing integrated business checking, merchant services, and lending relationships
Depositors wanting deposit insurance protection exceeding standard FDIC limits ($250,000+ through DIF)
diagnosis: Wrentham Cooperative Bank is best for Massachusetts residents and regional customers seeking traditional community banking with digital convenience, dual deposit insurance protection, and cooperative ownership. The main caveat is that specific rates, fees, and product details are not publicly available online, requiring direct contact with the bank, and as a regional institution it may have limited geographic reach and fewer ATMs than national competitors.
typical_results_timeline: Account opening typically takes 1-3 business days. Loan decisions vary by product.
